moveAnnotations.annotation: We play the Zukertort Opening, a flexible first move that sidesteps the typical 1.e4 and 1.d4 mainlines. By placing our knight on f3, we control the crucial central squares e5 and d4 without immediately committing our center pawns, giving us maximum flexibility in how we develop. This move is named after the legendary 19th-century grandmaster Johannes Zukertort, and it remains a favorite among players who prefer maneuvering positions over theoretical battles.

Black claims space in the center with 1...d5, immediately challenging our influence over the e4 and d4 squares and signaling intentions to fight for central control. By playing this move, Black avoids allowing us a comfortable space advantage while preparing to develop naturally with moves like Bf5 or Nc6. This is a principled response that tells us Black isn't afraid to contest the center right away.
